sound_type: male-female pitch-different duet -- call-and-response between male (250-400 Hz, deeper, 4-5 notes) and female (300-500 Hz, higher despite larger body, 5-7 notes); near-continuous territorial vocal presence; the most complex nocturnal vocalization in the PNW forest; E=5 combined power
ecosystem: Territory, winter. Defended territory in varied forest. Pair duet for territory declaration and pair bonding. Peak: November-February.
season: Year-round resident. Duet peaks November-February during courtship and nesting. Earliest nester in North America: eggs January-February.
family: Strigidae (2nd consecutive: 135-136. Third species with double entries.)

coordinates.description: Reference for Great Horned Owl pair territory. Pairs occupy territories throughout PNW forests, parks, and suburban areas.

taxonomy_note.description: Second Great Horned Owl entry (first: degree 135, solo hoot, E=4). Third species with double entries after Pileated Woodpecker (108-109) and American Dipper (116-117). E=5 reflects the combined acoustic power of the paired duet -- more complex and higher-impact than the solo hoot. Size-pitch paradox: female is 25% heavier but produces a higher-pitched hoot. Second consecutive Strigidae (135-136).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: third entry total (Spotted Owl at 4, GHO solo at 135, GHO duet at 136). Two consecutive at 135-136.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Same as degree 135. Bubo from Latin 'eagle owl.' virginianus from Virginia.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bubo -- second entry (first at 135). The pair duet is the second Bubo contribution.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Third entry. Two consecutive at 135-136.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae continues at degree 136. Two consecutive entries establish the family's presence post-Anatidae.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Male: 250-400 Hz. Female: 300-500 Hz. Combined duet spans 250-500 Hz -- wider bandwidth than either voice alone.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Male: 300-380 Hz. Female: 350-450 Hz. The harmonic interleaving creates a rich combined texture.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Duet: male 250-400 Hz alternating with female 300-500 Hz. The overlap region (300-400 Hz) creates harmonic complexity.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual hoot series: 2-4 seconds. Duet exchanges: male call + 2-5 second gap + female response. Complete duet sessions: 1-10+ minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: PAIR DUET: Male initiates with 4-5 note 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo' (250-400 Hz). Female responds with 5-7 note higher-pitched version (300-500 Hz). Alternating call-and-response. Can accelerate to overlapping during intense territorial encounters.
song_metrics.note_types: Male territorial hoot (deeper, 4-5 notes), female territorial response (higher, 5-7 notes), overlapping crescendo (intense encounters), coordinated silence (listening for intruders between exchanges).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Call-and-response: male-female-male-female with 2-5 second gaps. Established pairs have tighter timing. The rhythm is a measure of pair quality and bond duration.
song_metrics.timbre: Male: deep, resonant, bass-register. Female: higher, slightly thinner but still resonant. Combined: a rich harmonic dialogue with clear pitch dimorphism. The two voices create a chord-like quality.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Combined volume exceeds individual E=4. Near-continuous vocal presence (alternating calls fill the gaps). The most powerful regular nocturnal vocalization in the PNW forest.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate to accelerating. Baseline: measured exchanges. Intensifying: faster exchanges, potential overlap. The tempo reflects territorial urgency.
song_metrics.context: Territory declaration (primary), pair bonding, courtship, nest-site coordination. Peak: November-February. The duet is both acoustic display and behavioral coordination.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal vocal variation across range. The duet structure (male-female alternation, pitch dimorphism) is consistent throughout the species' distribution.
song_metrics.key_property: PAIR DUET -- E=5 CALL-AND-RESPONSE. Male 250-400 Hz (deeper, 4-5 notes) alternating with female 300-500 Hz (higher, 5-7 notes). SIZE-PITCH PARADOX: female 25% heavier but higher-pitched. Most complex nocturnal vocalization in PNW forest. Third double entry (Pileated WP, Dipper, GHO). Second consecutive Strigidae. Winter nesting: earliest in N. America. Lifelong pair bonds. Territory fidelity.

plumage.overall: Same as degree 135. Mottled brown and gray. Prominent ear tufts. Yellow eyes. Facial disc. White throat bib.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Female larger than male (reversed sexual dimorphism). Otherwise similar plumage. In the field, size difference visible when pair perches together.

physical_description.overall: Male: 0.9-1.8 kg. Female: 1.4-2.5 kg. Female ~25% heavier. Both have ear tufts, yellow eyes, barrel body. Female's larger size visible when pair perches together.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: In the pair, the larger bird is the female. Voice confirms: deeper = male, higher = female. The size-pitch paradox is diagnostic.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the alternating hoots at night -- the deeper voice is the male, the higher is the female. When you hear two different-pitched hoots alternating, you've found a dueting pair.

nodes.title: Pair Duet -- E=5 Call-and-Response
nodes.description: Male-female alternating hoots. Male: 250-400 Hz, 4-5 notes. Female: 300-500 Hz, 5-7 notes. Near-continuous vocal presence. Combined acoustic power pushes E=4 (solo) to E=5 (duet). The most complex nocturnal vocalization in the PNW forest.

nodes.title: Size-Pitch Paradox -- Larger Female, Higher Voice
nodes.description: Female 25% heavier (1.4-2.5 kg vs. 0.9-1.8 kg) but produces higher-pitched hoot (300-500 Hz vs. 250-400 Hz). Reversed sexual dimorphism meets reversed acoustic dimorphism. A biological anomaly. The deeper voice is always the male.

nodes.title: Winter Nesting -- Earliest in North America
nodes.description: Eggs January-February. Incubation through ice storms and sub-zero temperatures. 30-35 days. Nest appropriation from other raptors. Duet peaks during nesting. Early nesting = first access to best sites and maximum development time for young.

nodes.title: Third Species with Double Entries (135-136)
nodes.description: Pileated Woodpecker (108-109: laughing call + drumming), American Dipper (116-117: song + dunking), Great Horned Owl (135-136: solo hoot + pair duet). Three species with two acoustically distinct contributions. Reserved for exceptional acoustic diversity.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Strigidae (135-136)
nodes.description: Two consecutive Strigidae entries after 17 Anatidae. The owl family establishes presence. GHO solo (135, E=4) + GHO pair duet (136, E=5). The engine has shifted to nocturnal forest ecology.

cross_references.relationship: DOUBLE ENTRY! Solo hoot (135, E=4) to pair duet (136, E=5). Individual to partnership. 'I am here' to 'We are here.' The energy step-up reflects the combined power and territorial significance of paired hooting.
DOUBLE ENTRY PRECEDENT! Pileated: laughing call (108) + drumming (109). GHO: solo hoot (135) + pair duet (136). Same pattern: two acoustically distinct contributions from one species.
DOUBLE ENTRY PRECEDENT! Dipper: song (116) + dunking (117). Three species now with double entries. Each represents exceptional acoustic diversity within a single species.
STRIGIDAE INTRA-FAMILY! Spotted Owl (4, E=3) and Great Horned Owl (135-136, E=4/5). The GHO is the primary predator of Spotted Owls -- an ecological predator-prey relationship within Strigidae.

PAIR VOCALIZATION PRECEDENT! Raven pair (101, E=4) and GHO pair duet (136, E=5). Both document paired vocalizations as acoustically distinct from individual calls. Coordinated communication between bonded partners.
